narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80.5% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group and significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, unlike the much higher mid-level deciles typical of students who passed. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, standing out sharply against the top-decile averages seen in successful bar takers. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since it was closer to the 3.24 average of those who succeeded, though not enough to offset the other influences. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of passers and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for them, these didn't carry enough weight here, and your LSAT score of 31 fell notably below the 37 average for those who passed. Compared to typical accepted students, who often enter with stronger early and later undergrad deciles plus higher test scores, your profile signaled higher risk in the areas the model weighs most heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—it's just how your specifics stacked up against historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the mid-30s range, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore bridge programs or other law schools with more flexible criteria. With focused improvements, you can reshape your profile for better odds next time.
